title: Deployment Self-Healing Behavior
description:
Observe the Deployment controller's reconciliation loop by deliberately deleting a pod and watching it get recreated automatically.

**Focus**: Understanding that Deployments continuously reconcile actual state against desired state — the core of Kubernetes self-healing.

This exercise reproduces a common production scenario: a pod crashes or is evicted, and the Deployment controller detects the deficit and spins up a replacement automatically.

## Common Pitfalls
- **Mistaking ReplicaSet for Deployment**: Deleting a pod managed by a Deployment does NOT reduce replica count — the RS recreates it immediately.
- **Scaling to 0 breaks health checks**: If you scale to 0 in production, all pods terminate — no graceful drain happens automatically.
steps: Create Deployment 'web-app' with 3 replicas of nginx:alpine
Delete one pod by name using kubectl delete pod
Immediately run kubectl get pods to observe the replacement pod being created
Confirm pod count returns to 3 (self-healing confirmed)
Scale deployment to 0 replicas and verify all pods terminate
Scale back to 3 replicas and verify pods restart
hints: Use kubectl get pods to find a pod name to delete
Run 'kubectl get pods -w' in a separate terminal to watch events in real time
Set the app label value to 'web-app' — it must match exactly between spec.selector.matchLabels and the pod template labels
kubectl scale deploy/web-app --replicas=0 terminates all pods without deleting the Deployment

editor_config.initial_code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  replicas: # TODO: set to 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: # TODO: use 'web-app'
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: # TODO: use 'web-app'
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: # TODO: use nginx:alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80

_solution.code_solution:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: nginx:alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
_solution.validation_script:
#!/bin/bash
kubectl apply -f solution.yaml

# Wait for 3 pods
echo 'Waiting for initial 3 pods...'
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app --timeout=60s
count=$(kubectl get pods -l app=web-app --field-selector=status.phase=Running --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)
if [ "$count" -lt 3 ]; then
  echo "✗ Expected 3 running pods, got $count"
  exit 1
fi
echo "✓ 3 pods running"

# Delete one pod
pod=$(kubectl get pods -l app=web-app --no-headers -o custom-columns=':metadata.name' | head -1)
echo "Deleting pod: $pod"
kubectl delete pod "$pod" --grace-period=0

# Wait for replacement
sleep 10
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app --timeout=60s
new_count=$(kubectl get pods -l app=web-app --field-selector=status.phase=Running --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)
if [ "$new_count" -lt 3 ]; then
  echo "✗ Self-healing failed — expected 3 pods after deletion, got $new_count"
  exit 1
fi
echo "✓ Self-healing confirmed: pod recreated automatically"

# Scale to 0
kubectl scale deployment/web-app --replicas=0
sleep 5
zero_count=$(kubectl get pods -l app=web-app --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)
if [ "$zero_count" -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "✗ Expected 0 pods after scale-to-0, got $zero_count"
  exit 1
fi
echo "✓ Scaled to 0 — all pods terminated"

# Scale back to 3
kubectl scale deployment/web-app --replicas=3
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app --timeout=60s
final_count=$(kubectl get pods -l app=web-app --field-selector=status.phase=Running --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l)
if [ "$final_count" -lt 3 ]; then
  echo "✗ Expected 3 pods after scale-up, got $final_count"
  exit 1
fi
echo "✓ Scaled back to 3 — all pods running"
exit 0
